title: WID.world Average National Income — USSR (historical; no top income/wealth share series available)
source_org: World Inequality Lab / World Inequality Database (WID.world)
time_coverage: varies by country (WID coverage typically starts 1800-1900; see data)
units: income/wealth shares as fraction of total (0-1); anninc in local currency units per adult (see WID_Metadata csv for exact units/currency per row)
license_terms: WID.world data is open access (see https://wid.world/methodology/ and site terms)
notes: IMPORTANT FINDING: WID.world's 'SU' (USSR) area code has NO sptinc (top pre-tax income share) or shweal (top wealth share) series at all — queried via countries-available-variables and confirmed empty for both sixlets. Only anninc (average national income per adult) is available for SU, with real (non-fabricated) observed/estimated values starting 1950 (82 of 656 requested cells non-empty; data before 1950 is genuinely absent, not a download error). This is a real WID coverage gap for the USSR, distinct from Russia (RUS), which DOES have full top-1%/top-10% income and wealth share series (queried separately as country-rus/) covering the Tsarist, Soviet and post-Soviet periods via researcher reconstructions (Novokmet/Piketty/Zucman methodology) — RUS is likely the better series to use for long-run Russian/Soviet inequality history; SU/USSR here is kept only for its distinct average-national-income series. Retrieved via WID.world's underlying data API (https://rfap9nitz6.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/prod/countries-variables-dl), the same endpoint used by the interactive bulk-download tool at https://wid.world/data/ (client-side public API key extracted from the site's own app.js). Variables requested: anninc_p0p100_992_i, anninc_p0p100_999_i (average national income per adult, two age/population variants; no sptinc/shweal variables exist for SU so none were requested). DataQuality column in the CSV encodes WID's own data-quality/estimation-method flag per cell (0=best available observed/interpolated, higher codes indicate more extrapolation/modeling — see WID_Metadata csv and https://wid.world/methodology/ for the quality-code legend). Empty Value cells are genuine missing data, not fabricated. The full mega bulk file (wid_all_data.zip, ~842MB) was skipped per size-discipline rule (>500MB); this per-country extract covers the specific inequality metrics requested.
